text: Featured here are several of the thoracoscopic instruments utilized.
As seen, the sliding arms allow the jaws to be opened fully despite the narrow capacity of the port.
Featured here is a custom-made suction device that allows for suction, dissection, and aspiration.
The port incisions are demonstrated here on a patient about to undergo right upper lobectomy.
In this video, right upper lobectomy is initiated by dividing the posterior pleural reflection in order to expose the bronchus.
Further dissection allows visualization of the takeoff of the right upper lobe bronchus as well as the level 11 lymph node.
The lung is then reflected posteriorly and anterior dissection begun by mobilizing the branches of the superior pulmonary vein to the upper lobe.
This dissection may be performed with scissors or with cautery or with a harmonic scalpel.
Further dissection is then undertaken to delineate these branches as well as to mobilize them away from the pulmonary artery lying directly posterior.
After mobilization, the upper-low branches of the superior pulmonary vein can be encircled by using a dissecting clamp.
Once this dissection is completed, the camera can be moved to the anterior incision and a straighter clamp passed to the camera port incision to replicate the direction of the linear stapler.
Subsequently, the stapler can then be passed across the superior pulmonary vein parallel
to the posteriorly located pulmonary artery.
After application of the stapler, the pulmonary artery dissection has begun.
The truncus anterior is easily visualized, as is the posterior ascending artery.
All lymph nodes in this area are swept up into this specimen, although they may also be dissected separately and removed.
The ongoing pulmonary artery is easily visualized and protected.
In order to complete this maneuver, minimal retraction is required.
Lymph nodes between the truncus anterior and posterior ascending artery are swept
into the specimen to allow access behind the truncus anterior for the placement of a clamp.
Once again, this dissection is performed with a curved dissecting clamp to mobilize the posterior ascending artery away from the truncus anterior and the upper lobe bronchus posterior to it.
The linear stapler is then passed across the truncus anterior and the stapling is completed.

Further dissection of the posterior ascending artery and the lymph nodes at the hilum is performed to expose both the takeoff of the right upper lobe bronchus as well as the posterior ascending artery.
In this patient, two posterior ascending arteries are present and they are encircled together.
Once again, the linear stapler is passed through the camera port as this represents the best angle for stapling and dividing.
It is important to remember to minimize traction once the stapler has passed across during the stapling process.
After completing division of the vascular structures, right upper lobe bronchial dissection can be undertaken to sweep the pleura and the lymph nodes into the specimen.
The section allows identification of the bifurcation to the bronchus intermedius.
Once this angle has been defined, it is easy to pass a clamp behind the right upper lobe bronchus and complete the mobilization process.
Finally, the linear staple is passed across the bronchus and the stapling completed.
The angle defined by placing the stapler through the camera port creates an adequate bronchial stump.
Once the bronchus has been divided, it is grasped with a grasper and the lobectomy is completed by stapling the fissures.
The horizontal fissure is stapled first.
and the lobectomy is completed by dividing the oblique fissure.
The use of the stapling device to complete the fissures rather than blunt or sharp dissection minimizes post-operative air leaks.
